title: Case on Priority to Oldest among Surviving Children of Person of Distinguished Service to State
text:
1Case on Priority to Oldest among Surviving Children of Person of Distinguished Service to 
State
[2024Hun-Ka12, 2024Hun-Ba287 (consolidated), April 10, 2025]
* First Draft
This case concerns the portion of the first clause of the first sentence of Article 13, Section 
(2), Item 3 of the “Act on the Honorable Treatment of and Support for Persons, etc. of 
Distinguished Service to the State” that designates the oldest child as the higher-priority 
surviving family member where no child has been designated through consultation and no 
child mainly supported the deceased person of distinguished service to the State. The Court 
held that this portion is nonconforming to the Constitution and ordered that it remain 
applicable until amended by December 31, 2026.
Background of the Case
Petitioner-Complainant (hereinafter referred to as “Petitioner”)’s father, the late K.B. 
(hereinafter referred to as the “Deceased”), was registered as a person of distinguished 
service to the State and passed away on January 29, 2013. L.I., the Deceased’s spouse and 
Petitioner’s mother, had been designated as the Deceased’s higher-priority surviving family 
member, but she passed away on April 12, 2019. The Deceased and the late L.I. had three 
children, and Petitio ner is the second child.
On January 29, 2020, the Director of the Incheon Regional Office of Patriots and Veterans 
Affairs designated the oldest child among the children as the Deceased’s higher-priority 
surviving family member on the ground that no consultation among the children had taken 
place and that Petitioner did not qualify as a child who had mainly supported the Deceased, 
and accordingly issued a decision refusing to register Petitioner as the higher-priority 
surviving family member of a person of distinguished service to the State.
Petitioner filed a lawsuit against the Director of the Incheon Regional Office of Patriots 
and Veterans Affairs seeking revocation of the above decision, and while her case was 
pending on appeal before the Supreme Court, Petitioner petitioned the court to request 
constitutional review of the following provisions of the Act on the Honorable Treatment of 
and Support for Persons, etc. of Distinguished Service to the State (hereinafter referred to as 
2the “Persons of Distinguished Service Act”): the phrase “one of the surviving family 
members in the same order” in Article 13, Section (2), Item 1; the phrase “mainly supported” 
in Item 2 of the same Section; and the phrase “the oldest person” in Item 3 of the same 
Section. On June 21, 2024, the Supreme Court granted the petition as to the portion of Article 
13, Section (2), Item 3 that gives priority to “the oldest child,” thereby requesting the instant 
constitutional review, and rejected the petition as to the remaining portions.
Thereupon, Petitioner filed the instant constitutional complaint with respect to the phrase 
“one of the surviving family members in the same order” in Article 13, Section (2), Item 1 of 
the Persons of Distinguished Service Act, and the phrase “mainly supported” in Item 2 of the 
same Section.
Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether the following provisions violate the 
Constitution: 
ⅰ) Article 13, Section (2), Item 1 of the Act on the Honorable Treatment of and Support for 
Persons, etc. of Distinguished Service to the State (amended by Act No. 16659 on 
November 26, 2019), the portion providing that one child is designated as the 
higher-priority person through consultation among the children (hereinafter referred to as 
the “Consultation-Based Designation Provision”); 
ⅱ) the same Section, Item 2, the portion providing that the child who mainly supported the 
person of distinguished service to the State is designated as the higher-priority person 
(hereinafter referred to as the “Main Supporter Priority Provision”); and 
ⅲ) the same Section, Item 3, main clause, the portion providing that the oldest among the 
children is designated as the higher-priority person (hereinafter referred to as the 
“Priority-to-Oldest Provision”). 
The provisions at issue read as follows.
Provisions at Issue
3Act on the Honorable Treatment of and Support for Persons, etc. of Distinguished Service to 
the State (amended by Act No. 16659 on November 26, 2019)
Article 13 (Priority Order for Payment of Monetary Compensation)
(2) Where two or more surviving family members eligible to receive monetary 
compensation under Section (1) are in the same order, the monetary compensation shall be 
paid in the following order:
1. Where, through consultation among surviving family members in the same order, one 
of the surviving family members in the same order is designated as the person to receive 
monetary compensation, the monetary compensation shall be paid to such person. In 
such cases, matters necessary for the method and effect of such consultation among 
surviving family members and other necessary matters shall be prescribed by Presidential 
Decree.
2. Where there exists no person falling under Item 1, the monetary compensation shall 
be paid to the person who mainly supported or raised the person of distinguished service 
to the State.
3. Where there exists no person falling under Items 1 and 2, the monetary compensation 
shall be paid to the oldest person; and where the persons in the same order are the parents 
of the person of distinguished service to the State, the monetary compensation shall be 
equally divided and paid, notwithstanding Article 12 (1). In such cases, matters necessary 
for the method of dividing and paying the monetary compensation and other necessary 
matters shall be prescribed by Presidential Decree.
Summary of the Decision
1. Justiciability of the claim challenging the Consultation-Based Designation 
Provision 
The Consultation-Based Designation Provision applies only where consultation has taken 
place among the children of a person of distinguished service to the State. In the underlying 
case, however, the children of the Deceased failed to reach an agreement through consultation. 
4Because the Consultation-Based Designation Provision is not applicable to the underlying 
case, the claim challenging this provision lacks relevance as a premise for adjudication in the 
underlying case and is nonjusticiable.
2. Assessment of the Main Supporter Priority Provision
Under the Main Supporter Priority Provision, a child who “mainly supported” a person of 
distinguished service to the State refers to a child who is recognized as having provided 
support at such a high level that granting that child the status of a higher-priority surviving 
family member is justifiable, based on a holistic consideration of factors over the entire 
lifetime of the person, including the person’s age, property and income, and the content and 
duration of the support provided by the child. Therefore, the Main Supporter Priority 
Provision does not violate the void-for-vagueness doctrine.
“A child who did not mainly support the person of distinguished service to the State but 
provided some degree of support” and “a child who provided no support at all to that person” 
differ only in degree, and neither reaches a level that would justify granting the status of a 
higher-priority surviving family member. Thus, it is difficult to regard the two groups as 
essentially different. Even assuming that the two groups are essentially different, considering, 
inter alia, that it is natural for a family member to provide some level of support to another 
family member in need, there are reasonable grounds for not granting such status to a child 
who supported the person of distinguished service to the State to a greater extent than other 
children but nevertheless failed to meet the requirement of “mainly supported.” Therefore, 
the Main Supporter Priority Provision does not violate the principle of equality.
3. Assessment of the Priority-to-Oldest Provision 
Among the children of a person of distinguished service to the State, there may be a child 
who is in particularly difficult economic circumstances. However, the Priority-to-Oldest 
Provision designates age as the final criterion for selecting the priority beneficiary without 
taking into account such individual circumstances. This runs counter to the legislative 
purpose of the Persons of Distinguished Service Act, which is to stabilize the livelihood of, 
and enhance the welfare of, the surviving families of such persons. Even if, due to fiscal 
constraints, the total amount of various forms of compensation must necessarily be limited, 
5that legislative purpose could still be fulfilled without imposing an excessive fiscal burden on 
the State if, within that overall limit, compensation were paid to the child with a greater need 
for livelihood protection. The living standards and economic capacities of the children can be 
converted into graded categories by taking into account their property and income, and it 
cannot be said that paying compensation under the Act on the basis of such categories would 
pose significant procedural difficulties. Therefore, the Priority-to-Oldest Provision 
discriminates, without reasonable grounds, between older and younger children of a person of 
distinguished service to the State, thereby violating the principle of equality.
4. Decision of nonconformity with the Constitution and order for continued 
application
If a decision of simple unconstitutionality were rendered with respect to the 
Priority-to-Oldest Provision, the statutory basis under the Persons of Distinguished Service 
Act for determining beneficiaries of various forms of compensation would be eliminated. 
Moreover, in removing the unconstitutional state of the Priority-to-Oldest Provision, the 
legislature retains discretion, within the bounds of the purport of the Court’s decision. 
Accordingly, it is reasonable to render a decision of nonconformity with the Constitution with 
respect to the Priority-to-Oldest Provision and to order its continued application until the 
legislature amends it, with a deadline of December 31, 2026.
* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
final_decision: nonconforming to the Constitution, constitutional, dismissed
